messages.content: in c++, i use template class AutoRegisterTemplate to do some automatic registration in class constructor. Suppose ModuleClass is the template parameter, for different ModuleClass , the constructor extract their `ModuleClass::Register` function pointer in a static std::map variable all_register_funcs.   Now i have this problem,  I write `static AutoRegisterTemplate<ModuleClassA> file_local_var` in a .cpp file my_module_a.cpp, hoping ModuleClassA will trigger it's constructor and inturn put ModuleClassA::Register pointer into the `all_register_funcs`. However, when I run the program, the .cpp file my_module_a.cpp was never linked, it seems that this cpp file was optimized away by linker. Is this a template thing? how do i avoid the problem and achive my goal of using the instantiation mechanic of template to do automatic registration.

This is not a template issue, but rather a problem with the linker not accessing your .cpp file. There are a few possible solutions to this:

1. Ensure that your .cpp file is included in the build process. Make sure that it is being compiled and linked by checking your build configuration.

2. Declare your template class as inline. This will make sure that the template code is generated in every translation unit that uses it, so the constructor will always be called.

3. Use the linker flag "-Wl,--whole-archive" to force the linker to include all object files in the final binary, even if they are not used explicitly. This will ensure that your .cpp file is included, and the constructor will be called.

4. Use a separate "register" function instead of relying on the constructor to do the work. This function can be called explicitly from your main function or from another initialization function, and it can register all the necessary modules. This approach also allows for more fine-grained control over initialization order.
`Declare your template class as inline` analyze the pros and cons of this behaviour.

Declaring a template class as inline has the following pros and cons:

Pros:
- Ensures that the template code is generated in every translation unit that uses it, so the constructor will always be called.
- Reduces the potential for linker errors and can simplify the build process.
- Can improve performance by reducing function call overhead, especially for small functions or those called frequently.

Cons:
- May increase the size of the executable, since the generated code will be duplicated in multiple translation units.
- Can increase compilation time, since each compilation unit has to regenerate the code for the template class.
- Can lead to code bloat, since every instance of the template class will generate a copy of the code, even if only a few of those instances are actually used.

Overall, declaring a template class as inline can be a useful technique for ensuring that its constructor is always called, particularly in situations where initialization order is critical. However, it should be used judiciously, since it can increase code size and compilation time if overused.
how to declare a template class to be inline
i did this, i got inline specifier is ignored when there are no declarators
in c++ if a cpp file is not used, it will not be linked. how to change this to make sure it always gets built.

are there better ways to do this, since adding global variable is not a good idea. and it violates the assumption that variable in the cpp file is not used. if you access the dummy_variable from main function, the cpp file is used.
is there a way to force link source files to produce static library using cmake script

Yes, there is a way to force link source files to produce a static library using a CMake script. 

In your CMake script, you can use the `set_target_properties()` command to set linker flags that will force the inclusion of specific object files in the final static library. Here's a sample CMake code that demonstrates how to do this:

```
add_library(my_library STATIC
  my_module_a.cpp
  my_module_b.cpp
)

# Force inclusion of object files in the static library
set_target_properties(my_library PROPERTIES
  LINK_FLAGS "-Wl,--whole-archive $<TARGET_OBJECTS:my_library> -Wl,--no-whole-archive"
)
```

This code creates a static library called "my_library" containing the source files "my_module_a.cpp" and "my_module_b.cpp". The `set_target_properties()` command is then used to set linker flags that will force the inclusion of all object files in the final static library, even if they're not used explicitly.

Please note that this technique should be used with care, and only when you have a good reason to force the inclusion of certain object files. If you can structure your code differently to avoid using this technique, that's usually a better option.

The code you posted appears to contain typographic quotation marks. CMake requires the use of ASCII quotation marks (`"`), so you'll need to replace the quotes in your code before using it. Here's the corrected code:

```
set_target_properties(my_library PROPERTIES
  LINK_FLAGS "-Wl,--whole-archive $<TARGET_OBJECTS:my_library> -Wl,--no-whole-archive"
)
```

This code sets the linker flags for the "my_library" target, using the `set_target_properties()` function. The `-Wl,--whole-archive` and `-Wl,--no-whole-archive` flags tell the linker to include all object files in the final static library, even if they're not used explicitly. The `$<TARGET_OBJECTS:my_library>` expression expands to a list of all of the object files associated with the "my_library" target.
name all the properties that set_target_properties can set

Here are some of the most commonly used properties that can be set with the `set_target_properties()` function in CMake:

- `ARCHIVE_OUTPUT_DIRECTORY`: specifies the output directory for static libraries.
- `COMPILE_DEFINITIONS`: specifies a list of compile definitions for the target.
- `COMPILE_FEATURES`: specifies a list of language features required by the target.
- `COMPILE_OPTIONS`: specifies a list of additional compiler options for the target.
- `CXX_STANDARDS`: specifies the C++ standard used by the target.
- `CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED`: specifies whether the target requires the specified C++ standard to be available.
- `INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES`: specifies a list of include directories for the target.
- `LINK_DIRECTORIES`: specifies a list of directories to search for libraries when linking the target.
- `LINK_LIBRARIES`: specifies a list of libraries to link against for the target.
- `LINK_FLAGS`: specifies a list of additional linker flags for the target.
- `POSITION_INDEPENDENT_CODE`: specifies whether the target should be built as position-independent code (e.g. for shared libraries).
- `RUNTIME_OUTPUT_DIRECTORY`: specifies the output directory for executable targets.
- `SOURCES`: specifies a list of source files for the target.

Note that this is not an exhaustive list, and there may be other properties available depending on the version of CMake you're using, and the specific target type you're working with. You can refer to the CMake documentation for a complete list of properties.
